one_liner: A 34-year-old female with a tall, thin body habitus presents with palpitations and a systolic click-murmur heard on routine exam.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient presents for evaluation of palpitations she has noticed intermittently for the past 6 months, described as brief 'skipped beats' that last seconds. She also reports occasional atypical chest pain that is sharp and fleeting. She has mild exertional dyspnea with vigorous exercise that is new over the past year. She denies syncope, orthopnea, or leg swelling. Her primary care physician noted a murmur on exam.
stage2.family_history: Mother has this condition prolapse. Maternal aunt had Marfan syndrome. No sudden cardiac death in family.
stage2.social_history: Non-smoker. Occasional wine with dinner. Works as a graphic designer. Regular yoga and jogging.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Thin female, tall stature (175 cm), no acute distress. HEENT: High-arched palate, no JVD. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; **mid-systolic click followed by a late systolic crescendo murmur best heard at the apex, radiating to the axilla**; **murmur increases with standing and Valsalva (earlier click, longer murmur)**. Pulmonary: Clear bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. Extremities: Joint hypermobility noted, no edema. Musculoskeletal: **Arm span exceeds height, arachnodactyly**.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Normal, no evidence of significant heart failure

imaging_results.result: Posterior mitral leaflet prolapse (≥2 mm displacement above the annular plane in the parasternal long-axis view) with moderate mitral regurgitation (eccentric jet), thickened and myxomatous leaflets (≥5 mm), LVEF 60%, mildly dilated left atrium, no significant LV dilation
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Confirms MVP with moderate MR; myxomatous degeneration is the underlying pathology

imaging_results.result: Normal sinus rhythm, occasional premature ventricular complexes (PVCs), T-wave inversions in leads II, III, aVF
imaging_results.clinical_significance: PVCs consistent with palpitation complaint; inferior T-wave changes can occur with MVP

imaging_results.result: Normal cardiac silhouette, no pulmonary congestion

stage3.key_tests_rationale: Echocardiogram is the gold standard for diagnosing MVP and quantifying MR severity. ECG evaluates for arrhythmias. CXR assesses heart size and pulmonary congestion.

stage5.final_clue: Echocardiography demonstrates posterior mitral leaflet prolapse with myxomatous degeneration and moderate regurgitation.

debrief.classic_presentation: MVP classically presents in young, thin females with palpitations, atypical chest pain, and a mid-systolic click followed by a late systolic murmur. The murmur moves earlier in systole and becomes louder with maneuvers that decrease LV volume (standing, Valsalva). Connective tissue disorders increase risk.
debrief.pathophysiology: In MVP, myxomatous degeneration of the mitral valve leaflets causes excessive tissue, redundancy, and elongation of chordae tendineae. During systole, one or both leaflets prolapse superiorly past the annular plane into the left atrium. This allows mitral regurgitation. The click occurs when the prolapsing leaflet reaches maximum excursion and the chordae snap taut.
debrief.treatment_overview: Mild MVP without significant MR: reassurance and periodic echocardiographic surveillance. Moderate MR: echocardiographic follow-up every 1-2 years, monitor for LV dilation or declining EF. Severe MR with symptoms or LV dysfunction: surgical mitral valve repair (preferred over replacement). Beta-blockers for symptomatic palpitations. Endocarditis prophylaxis is NOT routinely recommended.
high_yield_pearls: The click-murmur of MVP moves EARLIER with standing/Valsalva (decreased preload) and LATER with squatting (increased preload).
MVP is the most common valvular abnormality, affecting 2-3% of the population.
Myxomatous (Barlow) disease is the most common cause of primary mitral regurgitation requiring surgery in developed countries.
Mitral valve repair is strongly preferred over replacement when feasible, with better long-term outcomes and no need for anticoagulation.
Infective endocarditis prophylaxis is NOT recommended for MVP unless there is a prior history of endocarditis or prosthetic valve.
sources: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease — https://www.heart.org/en/professional/quality-improvement/guidelines-and-statements
Freed et al., Prevalence and Clinical Outcome of Mitral-Valve Prolapse, NEJM 1999 — https://www.nejm.org/
